cards.title: Confirmation Bias
cards.teaser: You don't evaluate evidence — you recruit it. Confirmation bias is the tendency to seek, interpret, and remember information in ways that confirm what you already believe.

runs.text: Confirmation bias is the most expensive bias in software engineering because it operates invisibly during the decisions that cost the most: choosing a database, evaluating a vendor, architecting a system. By the time you notice it, you've committed.
What it looks like in practice:

items.text: You've already decided on a microservices split. You read the first three sections of the DDIA chapter on distributed transactions and stop — you found the counterargument you were looking for.
Your team evaluates two databases. The one that matches your existing mental model gets a thorough proof-of-concept; the unfamiliar one gets a surface-level benchmark.
A PR review finds problems with a design you sponsored. You reframe each issue as a solvable edge case rather than a signal about the design's fitness.

runs.text: The mechanism is not laziness — it's cognitive efficiency. Your brain is trying to reduce the cost of decisions by using prior beliefs as filters. The problem is that when the stakes are high, the efficiency cuts come out of accuracy.
The corrective isn't skepticism turned inward. It's structure: assign someone to argue the opposing case before the decision is made. Make disconfirmation a deliverable, not an option.
The most dangerous form is when confirmation bias operates in architecture reviews. The sponsor of an approach and the evaluator of that approach should not be the same person.
Like a search algorithm that returns results matching your query even when the best result would require a different search term entirely.

cards.title: Anchoring Bias
cards.teaser: The first number you hear becomes the reference point everything else is measured against — even when that number was arbitrary.

runs.text: Anchoring is why estimates in planning sessions are systematically pulled toward the first guess in the room, and why capacity planning is distorted by whatever the current system handles.

The mechanism: when reasoning under uncertainty, the brain latches onto an available reference point — the anchor — and adjusts from there. Adjustments are almost always insufficient, because stopping feels right once you've moved some distance from the anchor.
In engineering contexts, anchoring shows up as:

items.text: Estimate anchoring.

The first person to guess a task duration sets the magnetic north for the rest of the discussion. Everyone else anchors to that number and negotiates in smaller increments than the true uncertainty warrants.
Capacity anchoring.
"Our current system handles 10k RPS" becomes the baseline for future planning. The question becomes "can we get to 50k?" rather than "what does the new workload actually look like?"
Budget anchoring.
A vendor opens with a price. Even after negotiation, the final number is shaped by that opening figure — you've been anchored to their starting point, not to your actual cost model.

runs.text: The corrective: generate your own estimate independently before hearing others, or expose the team to multiple reference points simultaneously. Pre-mortems and range estimating (best case / likely / worst case) also reduce anchor dependence.
In estimation sessions, have each person write their estimate privately before any are revealed. Simultaneous reveal removes the anchor that the first speaker would otherwise set.
Like a price tag — even if you know it's inflated, your perception of "a good deal" is calibrated to that original number, not to the item's actual value.

cards.title: Sunk Cost Fallacy
cards.teaser: Past investment is not a reason to continue investment. The sunk cost fallacy turns already-spent time and money into a justification for spending more.

runs.text: "We've already spent eight weeks on this rewrite — we can't abandon it now."

That sentence has ended more than one engineering career and wasted more than one engineering year. The eight weeks are gone regardless of what you decide next. The decision in front of you is: given where we are today, is continuing the right choice? The past spend is not data for that decision.

The sunk cost fallacy is persistent in engineering for a structural reason: the people who built a system are usually the people asked to evaluate it. Abandoning the system feels like admitting the original decision was wrong. It was wrong — or the environment changed — but the embarrassment of acknowledging that is priced into the continuation decision in a way that distorts it.
Common forms:

items.text: Continuing to patch a legacy system that has failed three migrations because "too much institutional knowledge is embedded in it."
Pushing a v2 rewrite to completion past the point where early signals show the architecture is flawed, because stopping would mean throwing away everything already built.
Keeping a vendor relationship beyond its useful life because the integration cost was high and switching now "wastes" that investment.

runs.text: The corrective is the zero-based question: if we were starting today, with what we know now, would we make this choice? If the answer is no, sunk cost is the only thing keeping you here.
"We've already spent X" should trigger a flag, not a continuation. Treat it as a prompt to apply the zero-based question, not as a reason.
Like staying in a bad movie because you paid for the ticket — leaving doesn't recover the ticket cost, but it does recover the next two hours of your life.

cards.title: Availability Bias
cards.teaser: The probability of an event is estimated based on how easily an example comes to mind — which means recent and memorable events are systematically overweighted in risk assessment.

runs.text: Availability bias is what makes the last incident the loudest voice in the room during architecture reviews and capacity planning.

If your database went down three months ago due to a misconfigured connection pool, connection pool failures will be treated as a top-tier risk — even if the probability of recurrence is low and other failure modes are more likely. Memorable events feel probable. Events that have never happened to your team feel improbable, even when base rates say otherwise.
In engineering risk assessment, this manifests as:

items.text: Post-incident overreaction.
A high-severity incident triggers engineering investment proportional to the drama of the incident, not to the probability-weighted impact of future occurrences.
Novelty blindspots.
Failure modes that have never hit your team get underweighted in architecture review — they're not available in memory, so they don't feel real.
Vendor risk miscalibration.
A vendor your team has used without incident for five years feels low-risk. A new vendor feels high-risk. The actual track records may be different from that intuition.

runs.text: The corrective is base rates. Before weighting risks by how vivid they feel, look up or estimate the actual frequency. Incident review processes that normalize all incidents — not just the dramatic ones — also counteract availability bias over time.
A failure mode that has never hit your system is not evidence that it can't. Availability bias systematically underweights what hasn't happened yet.
Like thinking plane crashes are common because they dominate the news — the coverage is proportional to drama, not frequency, and your risk model gets shaped by the coverage.

cards.title: Recency Bias in Technology Choices
cards.teaser: The newest tool feels like the right tool — not because of fit, but because it's fresh in your mind and surrounded by positive signal.

runs.text: Recency bias in technology selection is availability bias applied to the hype cycle. The tool that got a conference talk last month, the framework that appeared on Hacker News last week, the library a respected engineer mentioned on a podcast — these receive disproportionate consideration relative to their actual fit.

The mechanism is availability plus social proof: the tool is top of mind, and the visible signal around it is positive (you don't hear conference talks about projects that quietly failed six months in).
What this costs teams:

items.text: Operational immaturity.

New tools have immature operational tooling. Observability integrations, cloud-native support, on-call runbooks, community answers to production failure modes — all of these improve over time. Adopting at early maturity means absorbing that risk.
Ecosystem gaps.
A new database or framework may have excellent benchmarks and a compelling feature set, but the ecosystem of drivers, connectors, and integrations that makes it production-ready takes years to build.
Resume-driven development.
In the extreme form, technology choices optimize for the individual's portfolio rather than the system's requirements. The new tool is interesting; the reliable one is not.

runs.text: The corrective: evaluate fit before evaluating novelty. Define the system's requirements, failure tolerance, and operational constraints first. Then evaluate candidates against those criteria — including mature options that don't come with recent conference energy.
When a new tool enters a team's radar, create a two-column list: what the tool is good at, and what it requires operationally. The second column is where recency bias hides the cost.
Like buying the newest phone model before reading reviews — the excitement of novelty is real, but it's orthogonal to whether the phone fits your actual needs.

cards.title: Planning Fallacy
cards.teaser: Humans systematically underestimate how long tasks will take and overestimate how productive future-them will be — and this applies specifically to engineers estimating their own work.

runs.text: The planning fallacy, named by Daniel Kahneman, is the consistent pattern of projecting best-case scenarios onto future work while ignoring the base rate of how long similar work has actually taken. It's not dishonesty — it's a failure mode of inside-view reasoning.

Inside-view reasoning says: "I have a clear mental picture of this task, I can see the path to completion, and nothing in that mental picture is going wrong — so I estimate based on the path I can see." The outside view says: "How long did the last five tasks at this complexity level actually take?"
In software engineering specifically:

items.text: Integration time is invisible.

The mental picture of a task usually models the happy path. Wiring two systems together, handling edge cases, debugging unexpected interactions — these don't appear in the initial plan because they can't be anticipated, but they consistently consume 40–60% of the actual work.
Interruptions are discounted.
Estimates are made imagining uninterrupted work. Actual work includes code reviews, questions, incidents, and context switching. Estimates don't adjust for this.
Dependencies are optimistic.
"I'll have this done as soon as the API is ready" assumes the API will be ready. The plan doesn't model that the API might be two weeks late.

runs.text: The corrective is reference class forecasting: before committing to an estimate, identify the reference class (tasks of similar type and complexity) and use the historical distribution of that class to calibrate.
When a team's estimates are consistently 50–100% over, it's not a skill problem — it's an inside-view problem. Reference class forecasting fixes this faster than estimating harder.
Like packing for a trip in your head — you imagine the perfect set of items fitting perfectly, and then you arrive at the airport with an oversized bag and still forgot the charger.

cards.title: Hindsight Bias in Postmortems
cards.teaser: "We should have known" is almost always wrong — and when it drives postmortem culture, it poisons root cause analysis and prevents real learning.

runs.text: Hindsight bias is the tendency, after an outcome is known, to believe the outcome was more predictable than it actually was. In postmortems, it shows up as "obviously the connection pool was going to fail under that load" or "we should have seen this coming" — statements that feel true from the future but weren't true from the past.
Why it matters:

items.text: It misidentifies root causes.

If the conclusion is "we should have known," the corrective action becomes "be more vigilant" or "have better engineers." These are not actionable. The real failure was usually systemic — a missing signal, an invisible constraint, an assumption baked into design that was never made explicit.
It creates a blame culture.
If the postmortem frame is "who missed this?" rather than "what made this invisible?", people learn to protect themselves in postmortems rather than contribute to them. Future postmortems get worse.
It produces brittle correctives.

Corrective actions built on "we should have caught this" add process on top of the current system — more reviews, more checklists — rather than making the failure mode impossible or detectable earlier.

runs.text: The corrective is to reconstruct the event timeline from what was known at each decision point — not from what we know now. Retrospective narratives should be built only from information available at the time. A good postmortem asks: "Given what was visible then, was this decision reasonable?" If yes, the work is to make the invisible visible, not to blame the decision-maker.
Blameless postmortems are not just a cultural courtesy — they produce better engineering. Blame terminates the investigation at a person; blameless analysis continues to the system.
Like a detective story in reverse — when you already know who did it, every clue feels obvious, but the investigators at the time had the same clues and no answer.

cards.title: Interrupting a Bias Mid-Conversation
cards.teaser: Naming a bias in the moment is the fastest way to slow down a decision — and slowing down is enough to change the outcome.

runs.text: Recognizing a bias in a finished decision is useful for learning. Recognizing it while the decision is being made is where the leverage is.
The interrupt pattern has three steps:

items.text: Name it.

State the bias explicitly: "I think we're anchored to the original estimate — can we reason from scratch?" or "Is this a sunk cost argument?" Naming is enough to create the pause that allows the group to notice.
Slow down.

Ask for evidence rather than argument. "What data do we have that supports this direction? What data would change our minds?" A request for evidence shifts the group from advocacy mode to evaluation mode.
Ask for the outside view.
"What happened the last few times we made this kind of decision?" or "What would someone who hasn't worked on this system for two years think?" Distance is a bias corrective.

runs.text: What makes this hard in practice:

items.text: Naming a bias can feel like an accusation. Frame it as a process observation, not a personal one: "I notice we might be in sunk cost territory" rather than "you're falling for sunk cost."
The interrupt requires social permission to do. Teams that normalize it in low-stakes situations are better at it in high-stakes ones. Build the habit before you need it.
The engineer with the least context is often the best-positioned to name the bias — they don't share the anchors, the sunk cost, or the confirmation trail the team has accumulated.

runs.text: Make bias-naming a retrospective habit first. Once a team is comfortable saying "that was availability bias" after the fact, saying it in the moment becomes a short step.
Like calling a timeout in a game — you don't need to know exactly what went wrong, you just need to recognize the team needs a moment to think before the next play.
